This project provides a complete preprocessing pipeline for the FALCON Benchmark M2 dataset, which contains neural recordings from the primary motor cortex in non-human primates during finger movement tasks.
PPC_Finger (human posterior parietal cortex recordings during attempted finger movements)

This project provides a kind of preprocessing Data from: Nonlinear decoding models enable music reconstruction from human auditory cortex activity dataset in the way that raw signals are transformed into signals for model training. The dataset contains intracranial electroencephalogram (ECoG) data recorded from 29 patients with drug-resistant epilepsy while passively listening to Pink Floyd songs. The dataset contains preprocessed neural activity (high-frequency activity, 70-150 Hz), electrode coordinates (MNI template space), and auditory stimuli (raw waveform files, and auditory spectrograms at 32 and 128 frequency points). The HFA and the two auditory spectrograms were sampled at 100 Hz and temporally aligned (190.72 seconds duration).
Data Source: Using adversarial networks to extend brain computer interface decoding accuracy over time (Dryad dataset). Includes data from multiple monkeys across different tasks (ISO/CO/RT/PG).

This dataset is sourced from Zenodo Record 3854034, which contains invasive neural recordings from the primary motor cortex (M1) of non-human primates performing a center-out cursor control task. The neural data were recorded using a 192-channel Utah array, and the behavioral data consist of 2D cursor positions and corresponding target positions. Each .mat file corresponds to one recording session.

This project provides A way to preprocess the Data for: A high-performance speech neuroprosthesis dataset to transform raw signals into signals for model training. The dataset consists of 12,100 sentences spoken by a single study participant. We use the "competitionData" version of it, which is a simplified version of the sentence data formatted and partitioned for machine learning research. It contains "train" and "test" partitions for model development, and a reserved "competitionHoldOut" set for the speech decoding competition (labels will be released after the competition). We currently only use the train (8800) versus test (880) partition data. For each sentence, a transcript of what the participant was trying to say was provided, along with a corresponding time series of neural spike activity recorded from 256 microelectrodes in speech related areas of the cortex.

Allen Brain Observatory — Visual Coding Neuropixels dataset (NWB, downloaded via AllenSDK). This dataset contains 58 experiments, each with up to 6 Neuropixels probes covering cortical, thalamic, and hippocampal regions, and is designed for systematic study of the mouse visual system under passive stimulation. This workflow uses drifting gratings (8 orientations) as an example, showing the complete process of session selection, unit filtering, spike matrix construction, time binning, and data saving. The official documentation describes the 8 orientations and temporal/spatial frequency settings in detail.
Long-term Recordings of Motor and Premotor Cortical Spiking Activity During Reaching in Monkeys
The Finger_RL dataset contains single-neuron recordings from two tetraplegic human participants attempting individual finger movements.
This project provides a complete preprocessing pipeline for Visual Grating Task ECoG recordings from neurotycho.org.

Based on the requirements of the classification task, we only use the perceptionTraining subset (150 categories × 8 images = 1,200 natural images). This subset has undergone standard fMRI preprocessing in the original paper, including head motion correction, spatial registration, and global normalization, making it suitable for subsequent training-test splitting.

This project aims to conduct standardized preprocessing on the BOLD5000 dataset, extract fMRI features and perform dimensionality transformation, providing high-quality input features and corresponding labels for subsequent model training related to visual cognition (such as image classification, brain activity prediction, etc.).

This project provides a preprocessing method for the THINGS-EEG dataset, transforming raw EEG signals into model-ready data. The dataset contains high-temporal-resolution EEG responses from 10 participants to naturalistic object images, recorded using a Rapid Serial Visual Presentation (RSVP) paradigm.

This project provides a way to preprocess the EEG Motor Movement/Imagery dataset by transforming raw EEG signals into signals for model training. Only two subsets of the EEG Motor Movement/Imagery dataset were used in the project. The first subset contains only performed left or right hand motor tasks. The second subset contains only imagined left or right hand motor tasks. In both subsets, a total of 103 out of 109 subjects were used, and subjects 38, 88, 89, 92, 100, and 104 were omitted due to data annotation errors.

This project provides a complete preprocessing pipeline for the FALCON Benchmark M2 dataset, which contains neural recordings from the primary motor cortex in non-human primates during finger movement tasks. The goal is to transform raw .nwb spike train data into synchronized, windowed samples of neural activity and behavioral labels (e.g. hand position or velocity) suitable for training machine learning models.
Species: Macaca mulatta (Rhesus monkey) Number of subjects: 1
Recording Location: Neural activity was recorded using an electrode array implanted in the primary motor cortex (M1).

Experimental Task: Subjects moved two groups of fingers in response to visual target cues presented on a screen. Finger position data, derived from the state of the manipulator, is provided and has been normalized between full flexion and full extension.

This pipeline processes raw .nwb files through an automated workflow to generate standardized .npy samples ready for deep learning model training. 1. Initialization & Reading To begin the automated pipeline, place all raw .nwb files in the source directory and call the main control function: falconm2(data_root, save_root, window_size, stride). This function traverses the directory to extract neuron spike timestamps and continuous behavioral data (specifically finger positions and velocities). 2. Downsampling & Temporal Alignment (Spike Binning) after constructing the trial-level spike matrices, the pipeline invokes the bin_spikes_for_regression(spikes, bin_size) function. This step performs downsampling on the binary spike data using a fixed time window (bin_size). By calculating the spike count within each bin, the system ensures that neural signals are strictly aligned with continuous behavioral labels in the frequency domain. 3. Sliding Window Segmentation The aligned data stream is then passed to create_windowed_dataset_for_regression(data, label, window_size, stride). This stage utilizes the defined window length (window_size) and stride to synchronously slice neural spikes and behavioral data along the timeline. This transforms the continuous data into the required model input format: [num_samples, window_size, num_channels] 4. Quality Control & Data Export Upon completion of the segmentation, falconm2 automatically generates spike raster plots for selected trials and saves them as images for manual quality inspection. The final input features (spikes_.npy) and corresponding regression targets (reg_labels_.npy) are exported to the designated save_root directory. 5. Dataset Formats Neural Feature Data: spikes_.npy Each sample represents a 2D neural activity heatmap, serving as the input feature for the decoding model. Data Type: numpy.ndarray Dimensions: [num_samples, window_size, num_channels] num_samples: Total number of samples generated after sliding window segmentation. window_size: Number of time steps per sample (e.g., a setting of 20 corresponds to a 400ms temporal receptive field). num_channels: Total number of neural units/channels (derived from the number of spike units, e.g., 64, 96, etc.). Behavioral Regression Labels: reg_labels_.npy Each label array is strictly aligned with its corresponding spike sample along the timeline, serving as the target for regression. Data Type: numpy.ndarray Dimensions: [num_samples, window_size, label_dim] label_dim: Label dimensionality. Typically 2 (representing x,yx, yx,y spatial coordinates) or 4 (representing x,yx, yx,y coordinates plus vx,vyv_x, v_yvx​,vy​ velocity vectors).

Species: Macaca mulatta (Rhesus monkey) Number of subjects: Multiple
Recording Modalities: Simultaneous acquisition of Electrocorticography (ECoG) and eye-tracking data during task performance. No motion tracking was conducted.

Task Type: Passive Viewing Task. Stimuli: Six distinct movie clips. Subject State: The subject (macaque) remained seated in a head-fixed configuration to minimize motion artifacts and ensure the stability of visual stimulation. Feedback & Reward Mechanism: No food reward was provided. The subject was not required to perform any active behavioral interactions, thereby eliminating potential confounding effects from operant conditioning. Synchronized Data Acquisition: Neural Recording: Cortical ECoG signals were recorded at a sampling rate of 1 kHz. Behavioral Recording: Eye-tracking data were acquired at a sampling rate of 120 Hz. Synchronization & Constraints: Neural and behavioral signals were strictly synchronized via hardware/software triggers. The experimental design explicitly excluded limb motion tracking, focusing the analytical scope on the mapping between visual input, oculomotor behavior, and cortical neural dynamics.
This workflow processes 128-channel ECoG LFP data and converts it into a binned spike representation suitable for classification and pattern recognition tasks.
128-channel LFP .mat files (one file per channel) Task_info.mat file containing trial labels and stimulus sequences Movie_start_time.mat file containing stimulus onset times

Parameter Purpose Variable Name Default Sampling rate Original LFP sampling rate (Hz) fs 1000 Channels Number of LFP channels n_channels 128 Trial length Duration of each trial (seconds) trial_duration_sec 4 Target timesteps Timesteps per trial after downsampling target_timesteps 50 Low cutoff freq Band-pass lower bound (Hz) lowcut 20 High cutoff freq Band-pass upper bound (Hz) highcut 250 Filter order Order of Butterworth filter order 3 Spike threshold Mean ± 2 × Std. Dev. auto-computed - Refractory period Minimum gap between spikes (ms) refractory_ms 2 Bin size Temporal bin size (timesteps after downsampling) bin_size 10

This workflow aims to perform band-pass filtering, trial segmentation, and adaptive spike extraction on Local Field Potential (LFP) signals. After time binning, these signals are converted into a sparse tensor dataset (.npz format) suitable for emotion classification tasks. 1. Label Mapping & Trial Alignment The system first reads the stimulus sequence (MovieSequence) from Task_info.mat and strictly maps the 6 types of physical stimulus IDs into 3 categories of numerical emotion labels (0=Frighten, 1=Threaten, 2=Neutral). Simultaneously, it reads Movie_start_time.mat to obtain the start timestamps (ms) for each trial. The labels and timestamps are then truncated to their minimum common length to ensure absolute alignment in the number of trials and labels. 2. Signal Cropping & Filtering The system traverses the raw LFP_ch{n}.mat files for all channels and crops fixed-length (trial_duration_sec) signal segments based on the extracted trial start times. Subsequently, a 3rd-order Butterworth band-pass filter (20–250 Hz) is employed to eliminate low-frequency baseline drift and high-frequency ambient noise. Using the np.interp linear interpolation method, the signals are uniformly downsampled to the target timesteps (target_timesteps), completing the reconstruction from raw one-dimensional signals into a (channels, trials, target_timesteps) three-dimensional matrix. 3. Adaptive Spike Detection Based on the filtered signals, the system independently calculates the mean (μ\muμ) and standard deviation (σ\sigmaσ) of the signal for each channel in every trial. Adaptive bidirectional detection thresholds are set accordingly (Positive Threshold = μ+2σ\mu + 2\sigmaμ+2σ, Negative Threshold = μ−2σ\mu - 2\sigmaμ−2σ). The system traverses all timesteps; once the signal exceeds the boundaries, it is marked as a Spike (value of 1). Upon triggering, it immediately enters a set refractory period (refractory_ms) to prevent redundant detection. This step transforms continuous analog signals into 0/1 binarized features with a shape of (trials, target_timesteps, channels). 4. Time Binning & Aggregation To further compress the temporal dimension, the system aggregates the extracted 0/1 spike sequences into bins according to a set bin_size, counting the total number of spikes within each time window (Bin). For example, with a configuration of target_timesteps=50 and bin_size=10, the system reconstructs them into 5 Bins, finally outputting a dense count matrix with a shape of (trials, binned_time, channels). 5. Visualization & Export At the end of the pipeline, the system automatically generates Filtered LFP signal curves, label distribution histograms, Spike Raster Plots, and Binned Spike Heatmaps for data quality spot-checking. The processed final data will be unified and packaged, then saved to the processed/data_YYYYMMDD.npz file.

PPC_Finger (human posterior parietal cortex recordings during attempted finger movements)
Species: Human (Homo sapiens) Subjects: Single tetraplegic human participant

Recording Method: Electrical physiological signals were collected using a 96-channel Utah array (Blackrock Microsystems) implanted in the left posterior parietal cortex (PPC, PC-IP region) for electrophysiological recordings. Recording Content: The dataset includes offline sorted neuronal spike activities (Sorted Spikes) synchronized with structured trial behavioral metadata, which encompasses finger category, visual cue (Cue) location, and key timestamps (start and end times) for each trial.
Experimental Task: BCI finger press task (cue on screen, participant presses corresponding finger)

This workflow is designed to parse raw .nwb files and reconstruct them into standardized tensor data (.npy format) that can be directly loaded by deep learning models, specifically for motion classification tasks. 1. Batch Reading & Data Parsing Traverse all raw .nwb files in the specified folder and use the pynwb library to extract the core information needed for decoding: the spike train (units['spike_times']) containing the firing times of neurons for each channel, and the corresponding action class labels, i.e., the finger category (trials['finger']) for each trial. 2. Trial Segmentation & Binning Convert discrete spike timestamps into continuous sample point indices according to the system's sampling rate (FS). The data is then segmented and time-binned by trial, transforming sparse spike points into dense features. A 2D feature matrix with the shape [trial_len, Channels] is generated for each trial. 3. Quality Check & Visualization To ensure data quality and feature alignment accuracy in the preprocessing stage, the system automatically extracts the feature matrices of the first N trials (N_PLOT) for visualization. A raster plot is generated, with time bins on the x-axis and neural channels on the y-axis. The plot is then exported as a PNG file for manual quality inspection. 4. Feature Separation & Export After processing, the global data is separated into features and labels for persistence. The input features (spike count matrix with the shape [Trials, Time, Channels]) are saved as spikes_{date}.npy, and the corresponding output labels (finger category array with the shape [Trials]) are saved as cly_labels_{date}.npy for seamless use by downstream classification networks.

Three data splits provided for the FALCON Challenge: held-in-calib: Many trials; intended for decoder calibration. held-in-minival: Small subset of held-in-calib; for validating model submission format. held-out-calib: Separate sessions with fewer trials; for few-shot recalibration.

This pipeline is designed to convert raw neural recordings into standardized tensor formats suitable for model training, covering the entire workflow from data ingestion to feature and label construction. 1. Data Loading Utilize the pynwb library or the dandi Python client to directly read and load raw .nwb files from either local storage or cloud repositories. 2. Signal Preprocessing & Binning Precisely align the extracted neural spike sequences with specific behavioral event timestamps. Subsequently, perform fixed-step binning operations on the continuous timeline to discretize the data. 3. Feature Extraction Extract core neurodynamic features from the aligned and binned data. Specifically, calculate the Mean Firing Rate and construct Peri-Stimulus Time Histograms (PSTH) to capture trial-averaged neural responses. 4. Label Generation & Windowing Map individual trials to their corresponding discrete experimental conditions to generate supervised labels. Simultaneously, rigorously define extraction time windows relative to key behavioral events to ensure temporal consistency across samples.

Target Brain Region: The recording site is focused on the primary motor cortex (M1), a key source for extracting motor execution commands and limb control signals. Acquisition Setup: High-density electrode arrays implanted in the M1 region enable high spatiotemporal resolution sampling of motor intention-related neural activity.

Self-paced finger movement task using two finger groups: index finger and middle-ring-small fingers Targets displayed on a screen: center-out style or random target style Each session: 375 trials Behavioral events: target onset, movement execution, target acquisition

Neurodata Without Borders (NWB) Total sessions: 312, recorded over 303 days (~3.5 years) Each NWB file contains: units: spike times SpikingBandPower: binned multiunit activity ThresholdCrossings: thresholded spike events index/mrs_position/velocity: kinematics trials: trial-level metadata

This workflow is designed to extract and align neural activity and kinematic features from raw .nwb files, and convert them into a standardized .npz dataset suitable for deep learning regression models (continuous trajectory prediction) through sliding window slicing. 1. Data Loading & Trial Segmentation The raw files are loaded using the pynwb library to extract underlying neural signals (including threshold pulses and spike-burst power, SBP) as well as behavioral signals (finger position and velocity). The system precisely extracts the start and end timestamps for each trial based on the trials table, and independently slices the continuous recording into trials. 2. Feature Extraction & Alignment For each segmented trial, a strictly time-aligned 2D feature tensor is constructed: Neural Feature Matrix: Extract an input matrix of shape [time, channels] (supporting the conversion of discrete spike events into average firing rate or smoothed continuous features). Kinematic Label Matrix: Extract the corresponding output label matrix of shape [time, 2] (representing the 2D position or velocity of the finger). 3. Sliding Window Sampling To allow the regression network to fully learn the mapping between neural activity and short-term continuous motion, the system applies an inclusive sliding window extraction along the time dimension for each trial. By setting a fixed window length (e.g., 100 time points) and sliding step (e.g., 10 time points), neural feature windows and kinematic label windows are synchronously extracted in pairs along the time axis. 4. Tensor Assembly & Export All short-term sample segments generated by sliding window extraction for each trial are concatenated and stacked to construct a globally unified 3D dataset. The processed input features (XXX) and regression labels (YYY) are then packed and saved as a highly compressed .npz file, ready for direct loading and training by downstream neural networks.

This project provides a kind of preprocessing Data from: Nonlinear decoding models enable music reconstruction from human auditory cortex activity dataset in the way that raw signals are transformed into signals for model training. The dataset contains intracranial electroencephalogram (ECoG) data recorded from 29 patients with drug-resistant epilepsy while passively listening to Pink Floyd songs. The HFA and the two auditory spectrograms were sampled at 100 Hz and temporally aligned (190.72 seconds duration).
Subject: Patients with drug-resistant (pharmacoresistant) epilepsy. Number of subjects: 29
Recording Mode: Intracranial electroencephalography data (specifically, cortical electroencephalography (ECoG)).
Experimental Task: Passive listening to the song "Another Brick in the Wall, Part 1" by Pink Floyd.

This workflow aims to convert raw .mat intracranial electroencephalography (iEEG) data into a standardized .npz tensor dataset suitable for music reconstruction tasks. 1. Data Loading & Alignment The system first reads the intracranial electroencephalography signals from the raw .mat files and precisely defines the trigger locations for positive and negative samples based on the experiment's start time, as designed. 2. Sequence Segmentation Based on the defined experimental start points, the system performs equal-width segmentation of the continuous EEG signals. Each experimental segment is forced to be truncated into a fixed-length time window (precisely containing 200 sample points), creating standardized neural feature matrices and their corresponding event label sets for each subject. 3. Execution & Export By modifying the variable configuration in the process.py script, the system executes end-to-end automated preprocessing. The final generated subject feature matrices and associated labels are packaged and saved in a highly compressed .npz format file.

documents.s: Preprocessing and Sliding Window Dataset Construction

documents.t: Species: Macaca mulatta (Rhesus monkey) Number of subjects: Multiple
Recording Location: Blackrock 96-channel Utah array threshold-crossing pulses, multi-unit (threshold crossings).

ISO (Isometric Force Control): Cursor on screen controlled by wrist force, 8 random outer targets; CO (Center-Out Reaching): Move planar manipulandum to 8 outer targets; RT (Random-target): Sequence of randomly presented targets; PG (Power/Key Grasp): Grasp force control task with 3 rectangular targets.

Preprocessing Parameters​ Parameter Suggested Value Notes bin_size 20 ms Typically varies between 10-50 ms window 500-800 ms Covers the time span of motor planning stride 10%-50% of window Balances sample size and data independence Variables​ Spikes: spike_counts, spike counts per 1 ms bin (shape: channel × time). Behavior: force/position/EMG, etc. (This example script uses force → 2D). Task Labels​ ISO: Uses force (2D) CO/RT: Uses position or velocity (2D) PG: Uses FSR pressure signals (1D/2D)

Example archive: Jango_ISO_2015.7z → extract to obtain multiple .mat files. Key fields inside each .mat file (example xds group): trial_result: trial status code (82 = success); time_frame: timestamps in ms; spike_counts: channel × time spike counts (1 ms bins); force: 2 × time force signals (used as behavioral signal in this example); trial_start_time / trial_end_time: trial start/end time; trial_target_dir: trial target direction (degrees: -45). Note: Behavioral variable names may differ across tasks/monkeys. Map them according to README (e.g., position, velocity, or FSR force).

This workflow is designed to convert high-frequency neural spikes (1 ms sampling) and behavioral signals (e.g., force/velocity) from .mat session files into a standardized tensor dataset (.npz format) that can be directly used for deep learning regression/classification tasks, through unified downsampling and sliding window slicing. 1. Batch Initialization & Filtering The automated pipeline is initiated by calling the main control script nprmsce(data_root, bin_size=20, window=30, step=6). The system uses h5py to traverse and read the .mat files in the directory (extracting the xds/* keys). Behavioral labels are used to strictly filter out successful trials (trial_result == 82), and neural and behavioral signals are sliced according to the timestamp of each trial. 2. Binning & Alignment Due to the high frequency of the raw signals, the process will align and downsample the trial data with a fixed time window (e.g., bin_size=20ms), and normalize the movement direction labels to {0..7}: Neural Spikes: The function bin_spikes_for_regression(spikes, bin_size) is called to sum the spikes within each time window, converting the sparse matrix [Channels, Time] into a dense firing feature matrix [Bins, Channels]. Behavioral Signals: The function bin_behavior_for_regression(behavior, bin_size) is called to average the physical quantities (such as force, velocity) within each time window, outputting a matrix [Bins, Features]. 3. Sliding Window Segmentation To capture temporal dynamic features, the aligned data is passed to the function create_windowed_dataset. Using the set window length (window=30 bins, approximately 600 ms) and stride (stride=6 bins, achieving 80% overlap), a sliding window operation is applied on the time axis (trials shorter than the window length are discarded). The data is then reconstructed into a three-dimensional sample set with temporal receptive fields. 4. Tensor Export & Formatting After processing, each session file is independently saved as a .npz compressed file with the same name. The core exported arrays are as follows: features (XXX): The input feature matrix with the shape [N, W, C] (N = number of samples, W = window length, C = number of neural channels). labels (YYY): The regression target matrix with the shape [N, W, F] (F = behavioral feature dimensions, e.g., force_x, force_y).

This dataset is sourced from Zenodo Record 3854034, which contains invasive neural recordings from the primary motor cortex (M1) of non-human primates performing a center-out cursor control task. The neural data were recorded using a 192-channel Utah array, and the behavioral data consist of 2D cursor positions and corresponding target positions. Each .mat file corresponds to one recording session.
Species: Macaca mulatta (Rhesus monkey) Number of subjects: 3

Core Configuration: Most experiments recorded activity from the primary motor cortex (M1) with a total of 96 channels. Extended Configuration: Some experiments implemented dual-area synchronous recordings from both M1 and the primary somatosensory cortex (S1), with a total of 192 channels. Recorded Data: Includes offline sorted spike times (Spike times). Each physical channel can distinguish and extract up to 3 independent sorted units (Sorted Units) based on firing characteristics. Behavioral Signals and Kinematic Data: The experiment synchronously captures the subject's movement features through high-frequency sampling, including: Cursor Position (cursor_pos): Reflects the subject's real-time movement trajectory. Target Position (target_pos): Marks the visual target endpoint of the task, used to decode movement intentions.

Behavioral Task: Monkeys voluntarily control their movement velocity to reach for targets arranged in a grid (e.g., an 8×8 grid), with no gaps between grid positions or pre-movement delay. Experimental Task：Center-out cursor reaching task (center-to-target).
Subject: Non-human primate (NHP) implanted with a 192-channel Utah array.

The original dataset includes: Spikes: Sorted spike times for up to 3 units per channel. Cursor position: Continuous 2D coordinates. Target position: 2D coordinates indicating the current target location. Timestamps: Trial-aligned timing information.

This workflow uses preprocess.py to preprocess raw .mat data, aiming to generate a standardized binning and windowing dataset suitable for regression decoding models. 1. Spike Time Conversion The system first extracts spike times from all channels and units, merging the spike times of different units belonging to the same channel. The discrete spike times are then converted into a continuous spike count matrix, strictly following the sampling frequency of the behavioral signals. 2. Trial Segmentation The trial boundaries are dynamically defined by calculating the per-frame displacement of the target position. When the frame-to-frame displacement exceeds a preset threshold (> 1 unit), the system marks the beginning of a new trial. The continuous neural and behavioral data are then precisely segmented into independent trial fragments at these boundaries. 3. Time Binning & Alignment The segmented trials undergo downsampling and bidirectional alignment. Spike Binning: The total number of spikes within a fixed-length bin (default bin_size is 5 samples) is counted. Behavioral Binning: The mean of the cursor position is calculated within the identical bin window, ensuring strict alignment of neural signals and behavioral labels along the time dimension. 4. Windowing for Regression To construct training samples for the regression task, the system performs a sliding window operation on the aligned data (default window size window_size is 100 bins, and the stride stride is 10 bins). This step generates paired independent windows for each trial: Features: Spike count matrix with shape [window_size, n_channels]. Labels: Binned cursor position with shape [window_size, behavior_dim]. 5. Result Export After processing, each data session generates and exports standardized tensor files: Input features spikes.npy (shape [n_samples, window_size, n_channels]). Regression labels behaviors.npy (shape [n_samples, window_size, behavior_dim]). Additionally, the system automatically plots and saves raster plots (.png) for the first 3 trials to assist in data quality checks.

This project provides A way to preprocess the Data for: A high-performance speech neuroprosthesis dataset to transform raw signals into signals for model training. The dataset consists of 12,100 sentences spoken by a single study participant. We use the "competitionData" version of it, which is a simplified version of the sentence data formatted and partitioned for machine learning research. It contains "train" and "test" partitions for model development, and a reserved "competitionHoldOut" set for the speech decoding competition (labels will be released after the competition). We currently only use the train (8800) versus test (880) partition data. For each sentence, a transcript of what the participant was trying to say was provided, along with a corresponding time series of neural spike activity recorded from 256 microelectrodes in speech related areas of the cortex.
documents.s: Neuroprosthesis Data Overview and Processing Pipeline

documents.t: Subject: One participant with Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) and severe speech impairment (anarthria). Number of subjects: 1
Recording location: cortical regions associated with speech.

Experimental Task: This dataset comprises neural activity recorded during 12,100 spoken sentences, along with instructed-delay tasks designed to investigate the neural representations of orofacial movements and speech production. Neural Data: Time series of neural spiking activity recorded from 256 microelectrodes (e.g., dual 128-channel Utah arrays) implanted in speech-related cortical regions.
Data is provided in .mat format and can be loaded using MATLAB or Python (via the scipy.io.loadmat function).

This workflow aims to convert raw .mat neurophysiological data into a structured dataset suitable for language decoding models (such as Speech-to-Text tasks). The entire pipeline covers block normalization of neural signals, phoneme conversion, sequence alignment, and fixed-length padding, ultimately outputting a standardized .npz tensor file. 1. Data Loading & Normalization The system first reads the target subject's .mat file by calling the loadFeaturesAndNormalize(sessionPath) function. At this stage, the system performs strict block normalization (Chunk Normalization) on the raw high-frequency neural signals, extracting the processed neural signal feature matrix (shape: [time_steps, channel]), while also synchronously reading the corresponding sentence transcriptions (transcriptions) and the time step length for each trial (frameLens). 2. Text Cleaning & Phoneme Conversion The data then flows into the core data processing pipeline getDataset(sessionPath). To meet the supervised learning requirements of the language decoding model, the system will clean the extracted text sequence by removing leading and trailing spaces and standardizing the text, then accurately convert it into a phoneme sequence. This completes the initial mapping of neural signal features to phoneme expressions. 3. Sequence Alignment & Padding Since the pronunciation duration and length vary for different sentences, getDataset will perform strict alignment reconstruction on the extracted features and labels: Label Padding: The encoded phoneme sequences are uniformly padded with 0 to a fixed length of 500 to facilitate matrix batching. Sequence Length Coordination: The system will precisely calculate and retain the true feature time steps for each sample (timeSeriesLens), discard the actual phoneme length after removing padding zeros (phoneLens), and calculate the phoneme-to-time ratio (phonePerTime) for temporal alignment. 4. Execution & Export The user only needs to specify the dataDir (competition data source directory) and out_data (output path) variables in the process.py script, and running the script will trigger the fully automated processing. The final generated feature matrix and sequence length statistics will be packaged and saved in the data_SpeechBCI_preprocessed.npz file.

Allen Brain Observatory — Visual Coding Neuropixels dataset (NWB, downloaded via AllenSDK). This dataset contains 58 experiments, each with up to 6 Neuropixels probes covering cortical, thalamic, and hippocampal regions, and is designed for systematic study of the mouse visual system under passive stimulation. This workflow uses drifting gratings (8 orientations) as an example, showing the complete process of session selection, unit filtering, spike matrix construction, time binning, and data saving. The official documentation describes the 8 orientations and temporal/spatial frequency settings in detail.
Species: Mouse (Mus musculus) Number of subjects: Multiple
Recording Sites: Spanning multiple brain regions including the cortex, thalamus, and hippocampus

Standardized Stimulation Protocol: A standardized visual stimulation protocol was administered to each subject (mouse) to ensure temporal reproducibility across experiments and to facilitate the parallel mapping of visual responses across diverse brain regions. Characterization of Classical Stimulus Features: Stimulus Content: Comprised of Gabor patches, full-field drifting gratings, and moving dots. Experimental Objective: Aimed at precisely characterizing the tuning properties of individual neurons, such as orientation selectivity or spatial frequency preference. Ecologically Relevant Experiments: Stimulus Content: A series of complex natural images and natural movies were presented. Experimental Objective: To explore the synergistic interactions and population dynamics across different visual cortical areas within an ecologically relevant and realistic context.

Neurodata Without Borders (NWB) Parameter Example Value Description cache_dir ./allen_vc_npx AllenSDK cache directory; stores downloaded metadata and NWB files. sampling_rate 30,000 Hz Raw sampling frequency of the Neuropixels probe. snr_thresh 2.0 Signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) threshold for unit filtering; higher values ensure cleaner signals. target_area "VISp" Target brain region for analysis (e.g., Primary Visual Cortex, VISp). expect_num_bins 50 Time-dimension alignment parameter; uniformly segments each trial into 50 time steps.

This workflow leverages AllenSDK to process high-frequency recordings from the Visual Coding – Neuropixels project. The data undergoes strict session filtering, brain region selection, and time binning to be converted into a standardized .npy trial sample set suitable for visual stimulus direction classification tasks (8-direction drifting grating). 1. Cache Initialization & Session Filtering The system first establishes and reads manifest.json, using EcephysProjectCache to automate project cache management and obtain the global session table. During session traversal, entries containing "drifting_gratings" (drifting grating) stimuli are precisely selected, and invalid parameters caused by empty screens (gray screens) are automatically cleaned (where orientation == "null"). The remaining 8 valid stimulus directions (0–315°, with a step size of 45°) are strictly mapped to discrete classification labels from 0..7. 2. Unit Selection & Spike Matrix Construction Within the selected session, the system filters the target brain region based on specific brain area acronyms (e.g., structure_acronym == "VISp") and excludes low-quality units with a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) below the threshold (snr >= snr_thresh). For each qualified trial, the system extracts spike times within the [start_time, stop_time) interval, converts them to relative indices based on a 30 kHz sampling rate, and initializes a sparse spike matrix of shape [num_units_selected, num_samples] for each trial. 3. Time Binning & Alignment To meet the fixed sequence length requirements of deep learning models, the workflow processes the original 0/1 matrices using the bin_spikes_for_classify function for downsampling. The system dynamically calculates the time window size (bin_size) based on the expected number of bins (e.g., expect_num_bins=50), and performs zero-padding at the sequence end if the total length is insufficient. The discrete spikes are then transformed into dense count features with the shape [expect_num_bins, num_units_selected] by reshaping the tensor and summing over the time window dimension. 4. Tensor Assembly & Export By running the data_process.py script, the pipeline automatically traverses all sessions that meet the criteria. The system stacks the processed trial matrices along the sample dimension to form a global 3D feature tensor and packages it with a 1D label array into a data dictionary. The final results are batch-exported as independent files to the local cache directory using np.save.

Long-term Recordings of Motor and Premotor Cortical Spiking Activity During Reaching in Monkeys
Species: Macaca mulatta (Rhesus monkey) Number of subjects: 4

Implant Configuration: The experiment involves four rhesus monkeys, all of which underwent chronic implantation of high-throughput electrode arrays in the primary motor cortex (M1) or dorsal premotor cortex (PMd). Hardware Channels: A 192-channel electrode array was used for neural activity detection. Processing Modes: The recordings include multi-unit activity (MUA) under raw potentials, with two feature extraction - modes available: manual spike sorting or threshold crossing. Kinematic Data: The 2D position and velocity vectors of the cursor controlled by the subject are recorded in real-time. Task Logic: Key metadata is integrated, including task-triggered time points, trial success status, and other relevant task attributes.
Experimental Tasks: Center-Out (CO) task and continuous random target task

Neurodata Without Borders（NWB） Parameter Description Example Value FS Neural data sampling rate 1000 Hz BINNED_LEN Number of time bins per trial after resampling 50 DATA_ROOT Path to raw NWB files ./data/sub-C/CO SAVE_ROOT Path to save processed data ./out/sub-C/CO

This workflow is designed to process raw .nwb data into a standardized trial sample set suitable for neural decoding or regression tasks. 1. File Traversal & Data Loading The system traverses the target directory, specifically filtering and processing .nwb format files, while extracting the date information (YYYYMMDD) from the files to be used as the subsequent file name identifier. It then uses the pynwb.NWBHDF5IO interface to safely open and load the underlying data of the NWB file in read-only mode ('r'). 2. Trial Filtering & Label Mapping The core trial information is extracted from the loaded data, including the start and end times (start_time / stop_time), target direction (target_dir), and execution results (results). Trials marked as successful (results == 'R') are strictly selected, and mathematical transformations (such as tgt_dir_after / np.pi * 4 and rounding) are applied to precisely map the original continuous target directions to 8 discrete category labels (values ranging from [0, 7]). 3. Spike Conversion & Resampling The spike times for each neural channel (spike_times, in seconds) are read and converted into discrete sample indices by multiplying by the system's sampling rate (FS). For each segmented trial, the system initializes a zero matrix with shape [trial_len, n_channels], marking the corresponding spike positions with a value of 1. Then, the sparse matrix undergoes equal-width resampling, forcing it to align to a fixed number of time steps (with BINNED_LEN = 50 set). The final feature matrix has the shape [BINNED_LEN, n_channels]. 4. Data Export & Visualization The system automatically creates directories under SAVE_ROOT/YYYYMMDD and saves the constructed sample list in a compressed {date}.npz file. To ensure data quality, the workflow strongly recommends generating three types of visualizations for review: Spike raster plot ([time, channel]) Trial label distribution plot Resampled spike heatmap (Spike heatmap)

The Finger_RL dataset contains single-neuron recordings from two tetraplegic human participants attempting individual finger movements.

Core Brain Regions: Left Posterior Parietal Cortex (PPC, area PC-IP): Responsible for extracting high-level motor intentions, spatial mapping, and cognitive planning signals. Left Motor Cortex hand knob (MC hand knob): Targeted for recording primary motor execution signals specifically associated with fine hand motor control. Neural Sensing: Captures discrete neuronal spike activity (single-unit activity) via chronically implanted intracortical microelectrode arrays.
Task: Attempted individual finger movements (both hands)
Single-neuron spike data, trial information, finger labels
Raw data: ../data/sub-P1/*.nwb Processed output: ../out/sub-P1/YYYYMMDD.npz features: [Trials, Time, Channels], with time dimension binned to 50 labels: finger class for each trial

This pipeline is designed to parse and reconstruct raw .nwb files into standardized tensor data (.npz format) optimized for motor classification tasks. 1. Core Data Parsing The system iterates through all raw .nwb files to extract the three primary data streams required for decoding: Neural Spikes: Retrieves units['spike_times'] (neuronal firing timestamps across channels) and converts them into sample indices based on the system Sampling Frequency (FS). Temporal Boundaries: Accesses intervals['trials'] to extract the precise start_time and stop_time for each trial. Target Labels: Reads trials['finger'] to obtain the specific finger movement category associated with each trial. 2. Trial Segmentation & Matrix Construction For each individual trial, the system performs precise extraction of neural spikes based on the onset and offset timestamps. These spikes are converted into a 2D dense matrix with the shape [time_steps, channels], recording the exact spike counts at each discrete time point. 3. Downsampling & Temporal Alignment (Binning & Padding) To eliminate variability in execution duration across different trials, the pipeline invokes the bin_spikes_for_classify(spikes, expect_num_bins=50) function. Aggregation: This operation aggregates the variable-length feature matrices into 50 fixed time windows (bins), outputting a standardized [50, channels] matrix. Zero-padding: For trials with shorter durations, the system automatically applies zero-padding at the end of the sequence to ensure strict dimensional alignment across the entire dataset. 4. Tensor Assembly & Export All normalized trials are stacked along the sample dimension to form a global 3D feature tensor. Finally, the feature matrices and their corresponding behavioral labels are packaged and exported as a high-compression .npz file, allowing for seamless integration with deep learning training frameworks.

This project provides a complete preprocessing pipeline for Visual Grating Task ECoG recordings from neurotycho.org. The goal is to extract event-related segments from raw .mat files, perform preprocessing (e.g., common average referencing (CAR), downsampling), and save structured datasets suitable for neural decoding experiments. Reference: Neural Decoding Using Kernel-Based Functional Representation of ECoG Recordings
Species:Rhesus macaque (Macaca mulatta) Number of subjects:Multiple
The experiment synchronously recorded the subject’s cortical electroencephalography (ECoG) data and eye position information using a 128-channel implanted microelectrode array.

Experimental Task: Subjects were head-fixed with both arms restrained. ECoG signals and eye positions were recorded simultaneously. Visual Stimulation: A monitor was placed in front of the monkey, displaying a grating pattern moving in eight different directions. Stimulus events are encoded as analog values and can be decoded from the 129th channel. Trial Structure: The monkey was not required to fixate on the screen. Blank screens and stimulus patterns alternated every 2 seconds. Physical Parameters: The sinusoidal grating had a spatial period of 27 mm and moved at a velocity of 108 mm/s (4 Hz). The distance between the monkey and the screen was 490 mm.

This workflow uses the ecog_process_all2.py script to preprocess raw .mat files from the Visual Grating Task dataset. The goal is to extract high-frequency cortical electroencephalography (ECoG) signals, which are denoised, segmented, and downsampled, generating a standardized .npz trial sample set suitable for deep learning classification tasks. 1. Data Loading & Event Detection The system first uses scipy.io.loadmat to traverse and load all .mat files in the directory and reads the underlying matrix X: separating the raw 128-channel ECoG signals (X[:128, :]) and the corresponding event label sequence (X[128, :]). The function get_event_indices(mat_file_path) is then called to precisely locate the continuous start and end indices for two core events based on label values: blank (label value ∈ [300, 400]) and raster (label value > 100 and not within [300, 400]). 2. Signal Denoising & Segment Selection After the data enters the process_mat_file(mat_file_path) function, the multi-channel ECoG signal undergoes common average referencing (CAR) denoising (i.e., subtracting the mean of all channels np.mean(signal_data, axis=0) to eliminate common-mode noise). To avoid signal instability at state-switching edges, the system strictly filters out short segments (only keeping segments with a length ≥ 1920), and forces the backward shift of the time window for segmentation to [start+20 : start+20+1900]. 3. Resampling & Sample Assembly For the long signal segments containing 1900 samples, the system applies interpolation-based downsampling to compress the data into a fixed length of 300 time points, creating a tightly packed 2D feature matrix of shape (300, 128) for each independent event. During feature assembly, the system automatically generates corresponding integer labels for each time window (0=blank, 1=raster) and extracts readable label names and source file tracking information (file_info). 4. Tensor Export & Report Generation All independent samples extracted from the sessions are stacked along the high dimension and packed into a Numpy container visual_grating_task.npz. Meanwhile, the pipeline automatically generates a statistical review file processing_report1.txt in the data directory, detailing the total number of files processed, the total number of valid samples, the sample distribution ratio for each category, and the contribution of each raw file to the overall sample set, ensuring traceability of the data lifecycle.

Technique: Mesoscopic single-photon / wide-field calcium imaging, used to record fluorescent signals from the cortex of head-fixed mice. The spatial resolution of the images is adjusted to 128 × 128 pixels.

Neural Activity and Behavior Synchronization: Neural activity and behavioral performance are synchronously recorded to analyze the relationship between cortical dynamics and behavior. Behavior Definition: Binary labeling based on the mouse’s movement speed: frames with movement speed > 0.5 cm/s are defined as “running” state, while frames with movement speed < 0.5 cm/s are defined as “resting” state. Experiment Scale: A total of 64 experimental sessions, with each session lasting 10 minutes. The number of sessions for the 5 mice are 11, 12, 14, 15, and 12 respectively.
The calcium imaging frame rate is 30 frames per second (30 fps). Each 10-minute experimental session generates approximately 18,000 frames of image data.

Imaging Data: .tif (raw images) Behavioral Labels: .mat Mapping Configuration: .csv Statistical Parameters: .npy (mean and standard deviation) Preprocessed Data: .npz (for direct loading and reading by deep learning models)

This pipeline is designed to convert raw wide-field calcium imaging data and corresponding behavioral labels into a standardized time-series window format suitable for model training, covering the complete workflow from data loading, temporal alignment, feature normalization to windowing and saving the sequence. 1. Data Loading & Temporal Alignment The .csv configuration file is read to obtain the mapping between data IDs and mouse numbers, and the corresponding behavioral sequence (run/rest) labels are extracted from the .mat file. The tifffile library is used to load the raw wide-field calcium imaging data in .tif format. A tolerance-matching algorithm (allowing ±5 frame errors) is applied to handle mismatched lengths (truncation or padding), ensuring strict alignment between the number of image frames and the behavioral label sequence. 2. Frame-wise Standardization & Dimensionality Reduction The precomputed per-pixel mean and standard deviation for each experimental phase are loaded from a file. Each aligned frame of the image is processed with per-pixel standardization. Based on the setting (e.g., flatten=True), the standardized single-frame images are flattened along the spatial dimension, and the channel-wise average is taken, reducing the data to a one-dimensional feature vector. 3. Sequence Sliding Windowing A fixed time window length (T=30, i.e., 30 consecutive frames as one sample) is set, and the continuous feature sequence is sliced into sliding windows with a stride of 1, generating all possible continuous time-window samples. 4. Label Generation & Formatting Storage For each sliced time window, the global label of the window is determined by majority voting (i.e., the most frequent label) among all the individual frame labels contained within that window. Finally, the windowed feature tensor (calcium) and the aligned label array (label) are packaged, named according to the convention YYYYMMDD_Mouse#.npz, and saved in .npz format for direct loading and training by the model.

The 5 mice were strictly divided into training, validation, and test sets in a 3:1:1 ratio, generating a total of 20 model configurations to assess generalization performance. When reproducing this data or conducting new research, care should be taken to prevent data leakage. Based on the requirements of the classification task, we only use the perceptionTraining subset (150 categories × 8 images = 1,200 natural images). This subset has undergone standard fMRI preprocessing in the original paper, including head motion correction, spatial registration, and global normalization, making it suitable for subsequent training-test splitting.

fMRI (Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging): Each scanning session includes: Functional images (EPI): Covering the entire brain. Anatomical images (in-plane T2 weighted): Acquired using the same slices as the EPI. High-resolution anatomical reference images (T1 weighted): Scanned only once for each participant, used as structural references.

The experiment is divided into two main types of tasks: Image Presentation (Perception) and Imagery (Representation) tasks. Image Presentation Task (Perception Task): Participants view object images from ImageNet and perform a 1-back image repetition task. Training Set (perceptionTraining): Contains 150 object categories, with 8 images per category, totaling 1,200 natural images. Each image is presented only once (24 runs in total, with 3-5 sessions). Test Set (perceptionTest): Contains 50 object categories, with 1 image per category, totaling 50 images. Each image is repeated 35 times (35 runs in total, with 4-6 sessions). Imagery Task (imageryTest): Participants are asked to mentally visualize one of the 50 categories from the test set. There are 20 runs in total (3-5 scanning sessions), with each run containing 25 categories, and 10 samples per category. Participants are also required to self-rate the vividness of their mental imagery.

MRI Imaging Data: All DICOM files were converted to NIfTI-1 (.nii.gz) format using FreeSurfer’s mri_convert. The T1-weighted images have been processed with pydeface to remove facial features. Task Event Files: In .tsv format, containing detailed stimulus timestamps and behavioral records. ROI Masks: Binary mask images in .nii.gz format.

The dataset is organized at the participant level and contains the following main directories/session types: ses-perceptionTraining: fMRI data for the perception training set. ses-perceptionTest: fMRI data for the perception test set. ses-imageryTest: fMRI data for the imagery test task. ses-anatomy: T1-weighted anatomical reference images. sourcedata: Manually defined Regions of Interest (ROI) mask files. derivatives/preproc-spm: fMRI data preprocessed using SPM.

This pipeline is designed to convert preprocessed 4D fMRI time-series data into a reduced-dimensional feature tensor format suitable for model training, covering the complete workflow from data loading, extraction, alignment, feature engineering, to the final construction of the data structure. 1. Data Loading & Indexing Preprocessed 4D fMRI data (.nii.gz), predefined ROI mask files (.nii.gz), and event annotation files (.tsv) are obtained from the derivatives folder. The data is strictly organized by participant (subject), session, and run levels. A data index dictionary is built using regular expression matching to map participants to their corresponding data files. 2. Signal Extraction & Temporal Alignment Each participant’s ROI mask is loaded and binarized. The 2D voxel signal matrices for each ROI are extracted in a fixed order. Based on the fMRI sampling period (TR = 3.0 s) and the peak latency of the Hemodynamic Response Function (HRF), the stimulus onset is shifted by one TR for temporal alignment. Voxel signals for each trial corresponding to the aligned TRs are extracted and concatenated to form trial data with a shape of [total_voxels, TRs_per_trial]. The trial labels are standardized to integer encoding starting from 0. 3. Feature Engineering & Dimensionality Reduction The data for each trial at each time point (TR) is standardized using the StandardScaler, such that the data has a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1. Subsequently, Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is applied to reduce the dimensionality for each time point, and the results are integrated across all time points. The final feature matrix is constructed while preserving the principal component matrix and the proportion of explained variance. 4. Label Generation & Data Storage The processed data is stored by participant in .npz format. After loading the data with np.load, the feature matrix can be extracted using the fmri keyword with a shape of (trials, number_of_principal_components, TRs). Labels ranging from 0 to 149 (150 possible values) can be extracted using the label keyword. Additionally, specific label names, ROI information, and voxel count statistics can be accessed through label_names, rois, and roi_voxel_counts.

This pipeline is designed to convert raw EEG recording data into a standardized tensor format suitable for model training, covering the complete workflow from signal preprocessing, feature extraction, to label alignment. 1. Data Loading The load_data() function is used to traverse the specified directory by session ID (session_id) and participant ID (sub_id), directly reading and loading the data files stored in .npz format. 2. Signal Preprocessing & Segmentation The raw EEG signals are downsampled to a unified sampling rate of 200 Hz, and a 1 Hz - 75 Hz band-pass filter is applied to remove environmental noise and physiological artifacts. The continuous EEG signal is then strictly segmented into 4-second non-overlapping time segments, with each segment treated as an independent data sample. 3. Feature Extraction & Smoothing From each segmented 4-second segment, power spectral density (PSD) and differential entropy (DE) features are extracted for five classical frequency bands (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma). Then, a moving average or linear dynamic system (LDS) filtering method is applied to smooth the features, further reducing noise. The final result is a three-dimensional feature tensor (features) with dimensions corresponding to the number of samples, number of channels, and frequency band feature dimensions. 4. Label Generation & Synchronization The emotional categories (happiness, sadness, fear, neutral) induced by the movie clips viewed by the participant in each trial are precisely mapped to discrete supervised labels. The time windows of the data are strictly aligned with the labels to ensure that each 4-second feature segment has a corresponding emotional ground truth, resulting in the final synchronized one-dimensional label array (labels).

This project aims to conduct standardized preprocessing on the BOLD5000 dataset, extract fMRI features and perform dimensionality transformation, providing high-quality input features and corresponding labels for subsequent model training related to visual cognition (such as image classification, brain activity prediction, etc.).
Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) records the temporal distribution of BOLD signal intensity within multiple visually relevant regions of interest (ROIs: PPA, RSC, OPA, LOC, EarlyVis).

Visual Stimulus Task: In a single trial, a visual stimulus image is presented to the participant, with each image corresponding to a regressor (1s ON, 9s OFF). The experimental images are sourced from three categories/datasets: ImageNet, COCO, and Scenes (SUN). Each fMRI run consists of 37 visual stimulus images.
The processed final data is stored in .npz format files, which can be directly read by Python and deep learning frameworks such as PyTorch/TensorFlow.

The data is stored on a per-participant basis. After loading the .npz file using np.load, the following two keys can be used to access the data: fmri: This represents the 2D fMRI image feature data, with a shape of (trials, 16, 16). label: This corresponds to the target label data, containing three possible numerical values (with an overall distribution ratio of approximately 2:2:1): 0: COCO category 1: ImageNet category 2: Scenes category

This pipeline is designed to convert fMRI time-series data into a 2D feature tensor format suitable for model training, covering the complete workflow from data loading and preprocessing to the construction of 2D features and labels. 1. Data Loading and Parsing The voxel time-series data from the ROIs, after GLM analysis, is directly obtained from the BOLD5000 Release 2.0 dataset. The data is stored on a per-participant basis. The .npz format files are loaded using the np.load function, and the corresponding data matrices are parsed using the fmri and label keywords. 2. Signal Preprocessing & Segmentation The raw fMRI data undergoes preprocessing via fMRIPREP, which includes motion correction and spatial normalization, followed by the estimation of mean-centered β coefficients using a GLM single-trial model. The extracted complete time-series is then segmented into three parts based on the dataset of the images presented in each trial. Linear interpolation is applied to the segmented sequences to adjust them to a uniform length. 3. Feature Extraction & Transformation The Gramian Angular Field (GAF) method is employed to convert the 1D time-series data into a 2D matrix. Using the mark_img function, the GramianAngularField algorithm from the pyts.image library is applied to the time-series features (after removing the label column, dat.iloc[:,:-1]). Normalization and polar coordinate transformation (e.g., summation calculation) are performed, and the resulting image data is reshaped into a single-channel tensor format suitable for CNN input, with a shape of (samples, 16, 16, 1). 4. Label Generation & Mapping The ImgType field from the corresponding events.tsv file is extracted to generate labels. These labels are standardized by grouping repeated and non-repeated images into a single class. The string labels are then converted into numerical format for machine learning, extracted as dat['label'], and finally mapped into three discrete values: 0 (COCO), 1 (ImageNet), and 2 (Scenes). The data distribution ratio is maintained at approximately 2:2:1.

Participants: 20 participants (10 male, 10 female), aged 19-26 years (mean age = 22.5 years, standard deviation = 1.8). Conditions: All participants are right-handed, with normal or corrected-to-normal vision and normal hearing. Screening: Participants were screened using the Eysenck Personality Questionnaire (EPQ).
EEG (Electroencephalography): EEG signals were recorded using a 62-channel Neuroscan system. Eye-tracking: Eye movement parameters were synchronously recorded using the Tobii Pro Fusion eye tracker.

The emotion induction is based on 80 video clips (12 clips for each of the following emotions: happiness, sadness, fear, anger, disgust, surprise, and 8 neutral clips). Each video clip has a duration of 2 to 5 minutes. Each participant completes 4 sessions, conducted on different dates. Each session consists of 20 trials. Each session induces 5 of the 7 target emotions to avoid frequent switching between emotional states. Each trial is divided into two parts: Viewing the emotion-inducing video (with a 3-second countdown before and after the video). Self-assessment of emotions: At the end of the session, participants review the video and provide a continuous rating (between 0 and 1). A rating of 1 indicates that the target emotion was strongly induced, while 0 indicates no emotion induction. For neutral videos, a score of 0 is given for emotional fluctuation, and a score of 1 is given for maintaining a natural state.
The raw EEG signal has a sampling rate of 1000 Hz and is downsampled to 200 Hz before feature extraction.

aw EEG Data: Stored in .cnt format. Raw Eye-tracking Data: Stored in .tsv format. Feature Data: Stored in MATLAB .mat format. Auxiliary Information: Stored in .csv and .xlsx formats. Processed Data for Python: Typically packaged in .npz format for use with deep learning models.

This pipeline is designed to convert raw EEG recording data into a standardized tensor format suitable for model training, covering the complete workflow from data loading, signal preprocessing, feature extraction, to label alignment. 1. Data Loading The specific load_data() function is used to traverse the specified directory by session ID (session_id) and participant ID (sub_id), directly reading and loading the data files stored in .npz format for reorganization. 2. Signal Preprocessing & Segmentation The raw EEG signals are first visually inspected for bad channels and interpolated using the MNE-Python toolkit. Then, a 0.1 Hz - 70 Hz band-pass filter and a 50 Hz notch filter are applied to remove low-frequency noise and power line interference. The sampling rate is downsampled from 1000 Hz to 200 Hz. The continuous signal is then strictly segmented into 4-second, non-overlapping time segments, each treated as an independent sample. 3. Feature Extraction & Smoothing From each 4-second segment, differential entropy (DE) features are extracted for five classical frequency bands (δ, θ, α, β, γ). Subsequently, the features are smoothed using Linear Dynamic Systems (LDS) or a moving average filtering method to further reduce noise. The resulting three-dimensional feature tensor (features) has the shape [number of samples, number of frequency bands (5), number of channels (62)]. 4. Label Generation & Synchronization The target emotion categories (7 classes) for each video, along with the participant’s subjective ratings (0-1), are extracted as label sources. The features and labels are strictly synchronized according to the time windows of the video playback, ensuring that each 4-second segment has a corresponding emotional ground truth. The final result is an aligned one-dimensional label array (labels).

This project provides a preprocessing method for the THINGS-EEG dataset, transforming raw EEG signals into model-ready data. The dataset contains high-temporal-resolution EEG responses from 10 participants to naturalistic object images, recorded using a Rapid Serial Visual Presentation (RSVP) paradigm. Each participant completed 82,160 trials, covering 16,740 image conditions. The training set consists of 16,540 (image categories) × 10 (images per category) × 4 (repetitions), and the test set contains 200 (image categories) × 1 (image) × 80 (repetitions). Training and test stimuli were presented in a pseudo-random order, and target images were used to reduce blinking and other artifacts. EEG was recorded with a 64-channel EASYCAP (electrodes arranged according to the standard 10–10 system) and a BrainVision actiCHamp amplifier at 1000 Hz sampling rate, with online filtering (0.1–100 Hz) and referencing to the Fz electrode.

Acquisition Hardware: A 64-channel EASYCAP EEG cap (electrode layout following the international 10-10 system) paired with a Brainvision actiCHamp amplifier. Online Processing: During data acquisition, a real-time 0.1 Hz to 100 Hz band-pass filter is applied online. Reference Electrode: The Fz electrode is used as the online reference.

Core Paradigm: Rapid Serial Visual Presentation (RSVP) paradigm. Stimulus Content: Object images presented against natural backgrounds. Quality Control Mechanism: “Target images” are interspersed within the image sequence to control participant attention and reduce the impact of blinking and other motion artifacts on the EEG signals. Experiment Scale: Each participant performs 82,160 trials (the training set includes 16,540 image categories, and the test set includes 200 image categories).

Raw EEG data from 10 participants. The raw EEG data in each .zip file is organized into four recording phases and training/test partitions according to the following directory structure: /sub-01 │ └───ses-01 │ └───raw_eeg_test.npy │ └───raw_eeg_training.npy │ ... │ └───ses-04 └───raw_eeg_test.npy └───raw_eeg_training.npy

This pipeline is designed to convert raw EEG data and corresponding stimulus image data into a standardized tensor format suitable for multimodal model training, covering the complete workflow from EEG signal preprocessing, image feature extraction, to feature and label recombination and storage. 1. Signal Loading & Epoching Using the core function epoching(), raw EEG data is converted to MNE format and subjected to channel selection. Continuous data is strictly segmented into time windows (epochs) from 0 to 1000 milliseconds following stimulus onset, with baseline correction using the data from 200 milliseconds before the stimulus. All retained electrode signals are downsampled to 250 Hz and categorized and sorted based on the image condition that evoked the EEG signal. 2. Signal Denoising & Whitening The mvnn() function is used to apply Multivariate Noise Normalization (MVNN) to both the training and testing sets. By calculating the covariance matrix of the EEG data and taking the square root of its inverse matrix (whitening matrix), the signal is transformed to eliminate inter-channel correlations. Additionally, for all EEG trials corresponding to repeatedly presented stimuli, averaging is performed to effectively improve the signal-to-noise ratio of the EEG image. 3. Image Preprocessing & Feature Extraction Stimulus images that induce emotion are spatially adjusted, uniformly scaled to a 224×224 pixel size, and normalized. The images are then input into the pre-trained CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining) model’s image encoder to extract high-dimensional image visual feature maps. 4. Data Integration & Storage After processing with multiple scripts (processing.py, obtain_feature_maps_clip.py, processed.py), the preprocessed EEG data and image feature data are saved by participant (e.g., sub01), divided into training and testing sets, in .npz format. Upon loading, the EEG feature tensor with shape (number of samples, number of channels, number of time points), the image feature tensor with shape (number of samples, features), and the strictly aligned image category label tensor with shape (number of samples, labels) are readily available.

This project provides a way to preprocess the EEG Motor Movement/Imagery dataset by transforming raw EEG signals into signals for model training. Only two subsets of the EEG Motor Movement/Imagery dataset were used in the project. The first subset contains only performed left or right hand motor tasks. The second subset contains only imagined left or right hand motor tasks. In both subsets, a total of 103 out of 109 subjects were used, and subjects 38, 88, 89, 92, 100, and 104 were omitted due to data annotation errors.

The BCI2000 system was used to record 64-channel EEG signals. The physical electrode layout strictly follows the international 10-10 system (excluding peripheral or specific electrodes such as Nz, F9, F10, FT9, FT10, A1, A2, TP9, TP10, P9, and P10).

The experiment primarily focuses on Motor Execution and Motor Imagery. Participants are required to respond according to target cues displayed on the screen. Each participant must complete 14 experimental runs, which include: Baseline state: 2 runs of 1 minute each (eyes closed resting, eyes open resting). Task state: 12 runs of 2 minutes each (the following 4 tasks are repeated 3 times): Task 1: Execute opening and closing of the left or right fist based on left-right cues displayed on the screen. Task 2: Imagine opening and closing the left or right fist based on left-right cues displayed on the screen. Task 3: Execute opening and closing of both fists (up) or both feet (down) based on up-down cues displayed on the screen. Task 4: Imagine opening and closing both fists (up) or both feet (down) based on up-down cues displayed on the screen.
160 Hz (i.e., 160 data samples are collected per second per channel).

Original Data Format: The data is provided in EDF+ format (containing 64 EEG signal channels and 1 event annotation channel), accompanied by an .event annotation file for parsing. Preprocessed Format: After filtering and segmenting, the data is outputted in the commonly used NumPy array format for machine learning, and saved in a compressed .npz file (e.g., EMM_task1_preprocessed.npz) for easy direct use in code.

This pipeline is designed to convert raw EEG recording data into a standardized tensor format suitable for model training, covering the complete workflow from data loading to feature and label construction. 1. Data Loading The core function load_data() is used to directly read and load the 64-channel raw EEG experimental data from 109 volunteers, recorded by the BCI2000 system, from the local folder. 2. Signal Preprocessing & Chunking A notch filter is applied to the EEG signal to eliminate 60 Hz power line noise, followed by band-pass filtering between 2 Hz and 60 Hz. The experimental data is then strictly truncated into 4-second segments (at 160 Hz sampling rate, resulting in 640 samples). Using a sliding window method, the data is further divided into 8 non-overlapping time segments (each window contains 80 samples). 3. Feature Extraction Features are extracted from the filtered and chunked data, and formatted into a NumPy array that can be processed by both CPU and GPU. A feature matrix with the shape (number of samples, number of channels, number of time points) is constructed and saved in .npz format to the output folder. 4. Label Generation & Mapping Each segmented window is precisely mapped to its corresponding experimental condition, receiving the same target labels as the original trials (e.g., representing the type of movement and whether the movement is for the left or right hand). The final supervised label array is generated and aligned with the shape (number of samples, labels).

LFADS (Latent Factor Analysis via Dynamical Systems) is a Variational Sequential Autoencoder specifically designed for neuroscience data. In the fields of Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI) and computational neuroscience, single-trial neural spike data is often accompanied by extremely high Poisson noise. LFADS breaks through the limitations of traditional Gaussian smoothing or trial-averaging methods. Its core hypothesis is that complex neural population activity is driven by a low-dimensional, non-linear intrinsic dynamical system. From the perspective of theoretical mechanisms, the core architecture of LFADS consists of the following five key components: Variational Sequential Autoencoder LFADS deeply integrates the powerful temporal modeling capabilities of Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) with the probabilistic framework of Variational Inference. Instead of outputting a single deterministic trajectory, it learns the posterior probability distribution of latent variables, thereby achieving unprecedented robustness and generalization capabilities in highly noisy single-spike data. Initial Condition Inference At the onset of every motor or cognitive task, the brain's dynamical system resides in a specific initial state. LFADS utilizes a backward-running RNN encoder (IC Encoder) to read the historical information of the entire neural sequence, accurately inferring the initial condition that drives the dynamical evolution of the current trial, along with its distribution probability. Autonomous Dynamical Generator Serving as the core state transition module of LFADS, the generator (typically a multi-layer RNN) receives the initial conditions and autonomously evolves forward in time based on its learned non-linear dynamical rules. This process effectively characterizes the intrinsic low-dimensional manifold properties exhibited by the motor cortex during action execution. Inferred Inputs & Controller To address unpredictable external stimuli or feedback inputs from unrecorded brain regions during brain activity, LFADS innovatively introduces a Controller Input Encoder (CI Encoder) and an RNN Controller. These two modules dynamically infer external perturbations at each time step and inject them as time-varying inputs into the generator in real-time. This significantly enhances the model's ability to fit non-autonomous, sudden neural activities. Poisson Emission & Firing Rate Reconstruction The final output of LFADS does not directly fit discrete spike sequences. Instead, it maps the high-dimensional states of the generator to low-dimensional Latent Factors, and subsequently uses exponential mapping (to ensure non-negativity) to reconstruct the underlying true and smooth neuron Firing Rates. The model employs Poisson Negative Log-Likelihood as its reconstruction target, strictly aligning with the statistical physics laws of neural spike emission.

As an unsupervised generative model, the preprocessing of LFADS input data must strictly adhere to neurophysical standards: Spike Binning & Tensorization: The input data must consist of discrete Spike Counts, rather than smoothed continuous signals. The time series of neuron firings are typically divided into fixed time windows, constructing a three-dimensional tensor containing trials, time steps, and neuron channels. Coordinated Dropout: This is the core feature engineering mechanism that guarantees LFADS's generalization ability. During the training phase, the model randomly masks the spike data of certain neurons in the input tensor, forcing the generator to rely solely on the Population Dynamics of the remaining neurons to infer the firing rates of the masked ones. This effectively prevents the autoencoder from degrading into a meaningless Identity Mapping. Trial Alignment: To ensure that "Initial Condition Inference" can effectively capture cross-trial variability, continuous EEG or spike signals usually need to be strictly aligned and segmented on the time scale based on specific behavioral events (e.g., target onset, visual stimulus presentation).
